seo.title: API documentation | Taimbox
seo.description: Taimbox integration API documentation. Connect your tools to planning, team, and project data.

toastLinkCopiedDesc: Paste it to share this section

intro.title: Integration API
intro.subtitle: Connect your agency’s planning, team, and project data in Taimbox with your internal tools.
intro.tokenBody: Each agency’s admins can create access tokens from API & integrations in the app. Each token is tied to your agency_id and protected by RLS (Row Level Security) policies.
intro.tokenRls: All operations are scoped to your agency’s data. You cannot access other agencies’ data.
useCases: Sync projects and employees with your ERP or CRM
Query planned vs. actual hours by week or month
Create absences and team events automatically
Build custom reports from planning data
Receive live updates via Realtime subscriptions

auth.title: Authentication & security
auth.intro: Every request needs two header values:
auth.apikeyBody: The anonymous key (ANON_KEY) for your Supabase instance—the same one the web app uses. Send it in the apikey header.
auth.authBody: JWT token created in <0>API & integrations. It contains your agency’s agency_id. Send it as Bearer <token>.
steps: Sign in to Taimbox as an admin of your agency.
Open Settings → API & integrations in the sidebar.
Click Create token, pick a clear name, and choose read-only or read/write permissions. Read-only tokens return 403 on POST, PATCH, and DELETE.
Copy the JWT shown. It is shown only once—store it somewhere safe.

security.title: Do not expose tokens
security.text: Never put tokens in public repos, logs, or client-side code.

security.title: Rotate regularly
security.text: Revoke and reissue tokens periodically. Revoke immediately if you suspect compromise.

security.title: Least privilege
security.text: Create tokens with only the permissions you need. Use read-only when you do not write.

security.title: Use expiry
security.text: When creating a token you can set expiry days. Non-expiring tokens are riskier.

auth.rlsBody: RLS policies ensure you only access your agency’s data. The JWT carries agency_id and PostgREST enforces it automatically.
auth.rateBody: There are no hard limits yet, but stay under ~100 requests/minute. Formal limits may be added later.

baseUrl.title: Base URL & headers
baseUrl.intro: All requests go to the Taimbox API URL. You need your Supabase ANON_KEY (same as the app) and an API_TOKEN from API & integrations.
baseUrl.codeBlock:
# Taimbox API base URL
https://api.taimbox.com/rest/v1/

# Required headers on every request
apikey: <ANON_KEY>                      # Anonymous key for your Supabase project
Authorization: Bearer <YOUR_API_TOKEN>  # Token from API & integrations
Content-Type: application/json
Prefer: return=representation           # Return created/updated rows in the body
baseUrl.importantBody: Your token is tied to your agency via a signed JWT. You can only read and write your own agency’s data thanks to RLS. Revoke tokens from API & integrations.
baseUrl.agencyIdTitle: When do I need the agency ID?
baseUrl.agencyIdRead: On reads (GET): Never. The token already identifies your agency and RLS filters automatically. You do not need agency_id in filters.
baseUrl.agencyIdWrite: On inserts (POST): When creating employees, clients, projects, or other agency-owned rows, the JSON body must include agency_id. Copy it from API & integrations (Connection data → Agency ID) or call GET /agencies (you will only see your agency).

responses.title: Responses & errors
responses.intro: All responses are JSON. The API uses standard HTTP codes and structured errors. Always check the SDK’s error field or the HTTP status on raw requests.
responses.empty200Body: A valid GET (good token) may return [] with 200 when no rows match or RLS hides every row. You only get 401/403 for invalid tokens or missing permission. Treat 200 + empty array as “no results”, not an error.

formatCards.title: GET list
formatCards.desc: JSON array of objects. It may be empty when there are no matches or RLS hides all rows (still 200—by design).

formatCards.title: GET/POST/PATCH one
formatCards.desc: Single JSON object when using Prefer: return=representation.

formatCards.title: DELETE
formatCards.desc: No response body. Confirm status is 204.

changelog.title: Changelog
changelog.intro: API change history. Check here for new features, improvements, and possible breaking changes.
changelog.empty: No changelog entries yet. Changes will be documented here.

entries.title: Time tables with agency_id and documented RPCs
entries.description: Adds agency_id to time_entries, active_timers, and timer_sessions so you can filter by agency in the API. Documents active_timers and timer_sessions in the resource reference, and the log_timer_hours (log hours when stopping the timer) and get_team_active_timers (list team active timers) RPCs in the REST section.

entries.title: API documentation v1
entries.description: First public release of the integration API docs: resource reference, tutorials, JavaScript SDK, Realtime subscriptions, sample responses, and readonly/readwrite permissions.

entries.title: Per-agency API tokens
entries.description: Admins can create and revoke JWT tokens in API & integrations. Each token is tied to an agency_id, supports read-only or read/write scopes, and is protected by RLS policies.

filtering.title: Filtering, pagination & sorting
filtering.orderCode:
// SDK
const { data } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .select('*')
  .order('name', { ascending: true })
  .order('created_at', { ascending: false })

// HTTP: order=name.asc,created_at.desc
filtering.fullIntro: Fetch February 2026 allocations for an employee, sorted by week:
filtering.fullCode:
const { data, error, count } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .select('id, project_id, week_start_date, hours_assigned, task_name, status', { count: 'exact' })
  .eq('employee_id', employeeId)
  .gte('week_start_date', '2026-02-01')
  .lte('week_start_date', '2026-02-28')
  .eq('status', 'planned')
  .order('week_start_date')
  .range(0, 49)

// count = total matching rows (for pagination)
// data = allocation rows

realtime.title: Realtime subscriptions
realtime.intro: Stream changes without polling—great for dashboards that refresh when allocations or absences change.
realtime.noteBody: Realtime is enabled on core tables (allocations, employees, projects). Contact us if you need other tables.

sdk.title: JavaScript SDK (optional)
sdk.intro: Use plain HTTP (see REST) or, with JS/TS, the Supabase client for a nicer API.
sdk.noteBody: You do not need to add agency_id to every query: your JWT already carries it and RLS enforces it in the database.

rest.title: REST API (HTTP)
rest.intro: Call the API from any language. Every resource follows the same REST pattern.
rest.getDesc: List resources with optional filters. Supports select, filters, pagination, and ordering.
rest.getSdk:
const { data, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .select('id, name, role')
  .eq('is_active', true)
  .order('name')
rest.postDesc: Create a resource. Send JSON in the body.
rest.postSdk:
const { data, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .insert({
    employee_id: employeeId,
    project_id: projectId,
    week_start_date: '2026-02-17',
    hours_assigned: 8
  })
  .select()
  .single()
rest.patchDesc: Update one or more fields on an existing resource.
rest.patchSdk:
const { data, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .update({ hours_assigned: 16 })
  .eq('id', allocationId)
  .select()
  .single()
rest.deleteDesc: Delete a resource. This cannot be undone.
rest.rpcIntro: Use RPC functions to log timer hours or list active team timers.
rest.logTimerDesc: Stops the timer and logs hours: UPSERT time_entries, updates allocations.hours_actual, inserts timer_sessions, removes active_timers. Only the employee linked to the current user (auth.uid) may call it. Params: p_employee_id, p_allocation_id, p_hours, optional p_notes, optional p_date (defaults to today).
rest.teamTimersDesc: Lists active timers for every employee in the caller’s agency. Returns employee_id, employee_name, allocation_id, task_name, client_name, started_at. No parameters.

quickStart.title: Getting started
quickStart.subtitle: Within five minutes you’ll have your first request working: get a token, configure the client, and verify connectivity.
quickStart.prereq2: Node.js 18+ (for the SDK) or any HTTP client (cURL, Postman, …)
quickStart.nextText: Now that the connection works, learn how to

steps.title: Generate your API token
steps.description: Open Settings > API & integrations in Taimbox and create a token. Give it a clear name like “My integration” and pick read permissions.
steps.note: The token is shown only once—copy it to a secure place (e.g. an environment variable).

steps.title: Install the SDK (optional)
steps.description: If you use JavaScript/TypeScript, install the Supabase client to simplify queries.
steps.code: npm install @supabase/supabase-js

steps.title: Configure the client
steps.description: Keep credentials in config or env vars—never hard-code tokens in source.
steps.note: If you only use HTTP (cURL, Postman, Python requests, …), skip this step: use the base URL plus apikey and Authorization on every call. Jump to step 5 for a cURL sample.

steps.title: Make your first request
steps.description: List active employees to confirm everything works.
steps.note: 401 means bad ANON_KEY or API token. An empty array may simply mean no active employees yet.

steps.title: Plain cURL works too
steps.description: Without JavaScript you can hit the API directly.
steps.code:
curl -s \
  'https://api.taimbox.com/rest/v1/employees?is_active=eq.true&limit=5&select=id,name,role' \
  -H 'apikey: YOUR_ANON_KEY' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_TOKEN' | json_pp

syncTeam.title: Sync your team
syncTeam.subtitle: Read employees, create new members, and update schedules from your own systems.
syncTeam.nextText: With the team in sync, learn to

steps.title: Read every employee
steps.description: Fetch all active employees with their core fields.
steps.note: RLS scopes results to your agency—no agency_id filter needed on reads.
steps.code:
const { data: employees, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .select('id, name, email, role, default_weekly_capacity, work_schedule, is_active')
  .eq('is_active', true)
  .order('name')

// => array of employees
// [{ id: "e1-...", name: "Jane Doe", email: "jane@...", role: "Designer", ... }]

steps.title: Create a new employee
steps.description: Required fields: name, role, default_weekly_capacity, work_schedule, and agency_id (copy from API & integrations → Connection data).

steps.title: Update working hours
steps.description: Adjust weekly capacity or per-day hours when schedules change.

steps.title: Deactivate an employee
steps.description: Prefer deactivating over deleting to keep historical allocations.
steps.note: Inactive employees keep past allocations. Avoid DELETE unless you truly need to purge records.
steps.code:
const { error } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .update({ is_active: false })
  .eq('id', employeeId)

// Hidden from planning and active views

planning.title: Automate planning
planning.subtitle: Create and manage weekly allocations in code—great for sprint pre-planning or spreading hours automatically.

steps.title: Read existing allocations
steps.description: Fetch planned work for an employee between two dates.
steps.code:
const { data: allocations } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .select(`
    id, employee_id, project_id, week_start_date,
    hours_assigned, hours_actual, task_name, status
  `)
  .eq('employee_id', employeeId)
  .gte('week_start_date', '2026-03-01')
  .lte('week_start_date', '2026-03-31')
  .order('week_start_date')

// Result: allocations for March

steps.title: Create an allocation
steps.description: Assign an employee’s hours to a project for a specific week. week_start_date should be a Monday.
steps.note: Non-Monday dates still insert but may misalign the visual planner.
steps.code:
const { data: allocation, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .insert({
    employee_id: 'employee-uuid',
    project_id: 'project-uuid',
    week_start_date: '2026-03-02',
    hours_assigned: 16,
    task_name: 'Homepage layout',
    status: 'planned'
  })
  .select()
  .single()

if (!error) {
  console.log('Allocation created:', allocation.id)
}

steps.title: Spread hours across weeks
steps.description: Insert many rows at once to spread a monthly budget.
steps.code:
const weeks = ['2026-03-02', '2026-03-09', '2026-03-16', '2026-03-23']
const hoursPerWeek = 10

const rows = weeks.map(week => ({
  employee_id: employeeId,
  project_id: projectId,
  week_start_date: week,
  hours_assigned: hoursPerWeek,
  task_name: 'Monthly campaign',
  status: 'planned'
}))

const { data, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .insert(rows)
  .select()

console.log(`Created ${data?.length} allocations`)

steps.title: Update actual hours
steps.description: When work is logged, bump hours_actual.

steps.title: Monthly targets (deadlines)
steps.description: Set how many hours each employee owes a project in a month.
steps.note: budget_override replaces the project’s default budget for that month only. Null falls back to project.budget_hours.

reports.title: Export reports
reports.subtitle: Query planning data for hour, profitability, and team utilization reports.

steps.title: Hours per project per month
steps.description: Pull every allocation in a month and aggregate by project.

steps.title: Profitability snapshot
steps.description: Join hours with project budget and monthly fee.
steps.code:
const { data: projects } = await timeboxing
  .from('projects')
  .select('id, name, budget_hours, monthly_fee')
  .eq('status', 'active')

const report = projects?.map(p => {
  const hours = byProject[p.id] || { planned: 0, actual: 0, computed: 0 }
  const progress = p.budget_hours > 0
    ? Math.round((hours.computed / p.budget_hours) * 100)
    : 0
  const hourValue = p.budget_hours > 0
    ? (p.monthly_fee / p.budget_hours).toFixed(2)
    : '0'

  return {
    project: p.name,
    budgetHours: p.budget_hours,
    hoursPlanned: hours.planned,
    hoursActual: hours.actual,
    progress: `${progress}%`,
    hourValue: `${hourValue} EUR/h`
  }
})

console.table(report)

steps.title: Team utilization
steps.description: Measure each employee’s load to spot overload or idle time.
steps.code:
const { data: employees } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .select('id, name, default_weekly_capacity')
  .eq('is_active', true)

const { data: monthAllocations } = await timeboxing
  .from('allocations')
  .select('employee_id, hours_assigned')
  .gte('week_start_date', '2026-02-01')
  .lte('week_start_date', '2026-02-28')

const occupancy = employees?.map(emp => {
  const totalHours = monthAllocations
    ?.filter(a => a.employee_id === emp.id)
    .reduce((sum, a) => sum + a.hours_assigned, 0) || 0
  const capacity = emp.default_weekly_capacity * 4
  const load = capacity > 0 ? Math.round((totalHours / capacity) * 100) : 0

  return { name: emp.name, totalHours, capacity, load: `${load}%` }
})

console.table(occupancy)

steps.title: Export to CSV
steps.description: Serialize rows for Excel or Sheets.

absences.title: Manage absences
absences.subtitle: Record PTO, sick days, and team events so available capacity updates automatically.
absences.doneText: You now know the core API flows. Open the

steps.title: Read an employee’s absences
steps.description: List absences that overlap a date range.
steps.code:
const { data: absences } = await timeboxing
  .from('absences')
  .select('id, employee_id, start_date, end_date, type, hours, description')
  .eq('employee_id', employeeId)
  .gte('end_date', '2026-03-01')
  .order('start_date')

// March PTO / leave for this employee

steps.title: Create a vacation block
steps.description: Insert a multi-day vacation; capacity drops automatically in planning.
steps.note: Common type strings: vacation, sick, leave, training—but any text works.
steps.code:
const { data: absence, error } = await timeboxing
  .from('absences')
  .insert({
    employee_id: employeeId,
    start_date: '2026-04-06',
    end_date: '2026-04-10',
    type: 'vacation',
    description: 'Spring break'
  })
  .select()
  .single()

if (!error) {
  console.log('Absence saved:', absence.id)
}

steps.title: Partial-day absence
steps.description: Use the hours field when the whole day is not off.
steps.code:
const { data } = await timeboxing
  .from('absences')
  .insert({
    employee_id: employeeId,
    start_date: '2026-03-15',
    end_date: '2026-03-15',
    type: 'leave',
    hours: 4,
    description: 'Morning doctor visit'
  })
  .select()
  .single()

// Only 4h removed that day

steps.title: Team-wide event (holiday)
steps.description: team_events can target many employees at once.
steps.code:
const { data: employees } = await timeboxing
  .from('employees')
  .select('id')
  .eq('is_active', true)

const employeeIds = employees?.map(e => e.id) || []

const { data: event } = await timeboxing
  .from('team_events')
  .insert({
    name: 'Regional holiday',
    date: '2026-02-28',
    hours_reduction: 8,
    affected_employee_ids: employeeIds,
    description: 'Office closed'
  })
  .select()
  .single()

console.log('Event created for', employeeIds.length, 'people')

steps.title: Estimate real capacity
steps.description: Combine absences, events, and base weekly hours for a rough net capacity.
steps.note: Taimbox uses a finer daily model internally—this is a simplified illustration.
steps.code:
const weekStart = '2026-03-09'
const weekEnd = '2026-03-13'

const [{ data: absences }, { data: events }, { data: employee }] = await Promise.all([
  timeboxing.from('absences')
    .select('start_date, end_date, hours')
    .eq('employee_id', empId)
    .lte('start_date', weekEnd)
    .gte('end_date', weekStart),
  timeboxing.from('team_events')
    .select('date, hours_reduction')
    .gte('date', weekStart)
    .lte('date', weekEnd),
  timeboxing.from('employees')
    .select('default_weekly_capacity')
    .eq('id', empId)
    .single()
])

const baseCapacity = employee?.default_weekly_capacity || 40
const absenceHours = absences?.reduce((s, a) => s + (a.hours || 8), 0) || 0
const eventHours = events?.reduce((s, e) => s + e.hours_reduction, 0) || 0
const effectiveCapacity = Math.max(0, baseCapacity - Math.max(absenceHours, eventHours))

console.log(`Effective capacity: ${effectiveCapacity}h / ${baseCapacity}h`)
